6.name: Ekiben + Mt Fuji seat (E, right side)
6.desc: The bullet train from Tokyo to Kyoto runs the classic ekiben ritual: grab a station bento off the platform kiosk, then eat it at 175 mph. Book seat E (right side heading west) and Mt Fuji fills the window for a minute or two around Shin-Fuji, roughly 40 minutes out. Have the phone ready because it comes and goes fast.
facts: Seat E = right side, Fuji view
Reserve seats; Nozomi not on JR Pass

6.name: Kyoto International Manga Museum
6.desc: A former 1920s elementary school packed with 50,000 volumes of manga you literally pull off the wall-length shelves and read wherever you want, including on the front lawn. There are English shelves, vintage series, and rotating exhibits on the art form. Easily two hours if he falls into a series.

6.name: Teramachi / Shinkyogoku arcade crawl
6.desc: Two parallel covered shopping streets running off Nishiki Market, wall-to-wall with gachapon machines, claw-machine arcades, retro game shops, and cheap eats. It is the low-stakes place to blow a few hundred yen on UFO catchers after lunch. Fully roofed, so it works rain or shine.

6.name: Marufukuro (original Nintendo HQ)
6.desc: Nintendo's original 1889 headquarters, from its playing-card days, now restored as a boutique hotel. You cannot tour inside without a reservation, but the preserved facade is a quick pilgrimage photo for anyone who grew up on Mario. It sits in the quiet Kagimachi area south of downtown.
facts: Near Nintendo's old card factory district

7.name: Fushimi Inari full summit loop
7.desc: Most people stop at the Yotsutsuji viewpoint and turn back, but the trail keeps climbing through thousands of vermilion torii gates to Ichinomine at the actual summit. The full loop is about two to three hours and thins out fast the higher you go. It is a legit hike with a real payoff and city views on the way up.
facts: JR Inari or Keihan Fushimi-Inari station

7.name: Samurai armor / kimono photo walk
7.desc: Shops near Kiyomizu-dera rent full samurai armor or a yukata and send you out into the Higashiyama lanes for photos. The old wooden streets like Ninenzaka make an ideal backdrop. Peak main-character energy for a couple hours of walking.

7.name: Night return to Fushimi Inari
7.desc: The shrine never closes and the lower torii tunnels are softly lit after dark, with almost nobody around. It turns into a genuinely eerie, cinematic walk once the day crowds clear out. Bring a phone light for the upper stretches where lanterns thin out.
facts: Lower loop lit; upper trail dark

7.name: Ninja/samurai experience add-on
7.desc: Hands-on dojo sessions in the Higashiyama area teach katana-drawing (iaido) basics or shuriken throwing in about an hour. An instructor walks you through stances and a few cuts, and you get to actually handle the gear. Good energy burner before dinner if the legs held up.

8.name: Todai-ji nostril pillar challenge
8.desc: Inside Todai-ji's Great Buddha Hall, one wooden pillar has a hole cut the exact size of the giant bronze Buddha's nostril. Squeezing through it supposedly grants enlightenment, and it is an easy fit for kids but a proper wriggle for a teen. Getting half-stuck is the point; someone films it.
facts: In Nara Park, walkable from station

8.name: Uji 'Sound! Euphonium' pilgrimage
8.desc: Uji is the real-world setting of the anime Sound! Euphonium, and you can walk the exact riverbank, the Uji-bashi bridge, and the Keihan station used as backdrops. Even if he has not seen it, the riverside temples and tea shops make Uji worth the detour. Fans photo-match scenes shot for shot.
facts: JR or Keihan to Uji station

8.name: Shika senbei deer gauntlet
8.desc: Vendors in Nara Park sell shika senbei crackers and the semi-wild deer mob you the second they see them. Hold a cracker up and many will bow their heads first, then get pushy and nip if you stall. Chaotic, hilarious, and the definitive Nara photo op.

8.name: Byodo-in Phoenix Hall
8.desc: The temple pictured on the back of the 10-yen coin, its Phoenix Hall floats over a reflecting pond and dates to 1053. Pull a 10-yen out of your pocket and match it in person before hitting Uji's matcha shops. The on-site museum holds the original phoenix statues and bell.

9.name: Iwatayama Monkey Park
9.desc: A 20-minute uphill hike above Arashiyama lands you at a hilltop clearing full of wild Japanese macaques and a panoramic view over Kyoto. You feed them from inside a mesh hut, so you are the one caged while they roam free. Real wild monkeys, no fences between you on the trail.
facts: Feed only from inside the hut

9.name: Dotonbori as Sotenbori
9.desc: Dotonbori is the real-world basis for Sotenbori in the Yakuza games, so run the first Osaka night as a walking tour: find the Glico running man sign, the giant moving Kani Doraku crab, and the Ebisu-bashi bridge. Neon overload, canal reflections, and street food everywhere. Fans clock the geography instantly.
facts: Namba station, then walk to canal

9.name: Arashiyama e-bike loop
9.desc: Renting e-bikes lets you hit the bamboo grove, Togetsukyo bridge, and the quieter moss temples in one loop while beating the tour buses. The electric assist makes the low hills nothing. Best done early when the bamboo path is still empty.

9.name: Ebisu Tower Ferris wheel
9.desc: An oval-shaped Ferris wheel built into the Don Quijote store right on the Dotonbori canal, unusual because it moves sideways as well as up. It gives a quick neon overview of Minami before the takoyaki crawl. Short ride, big first-night payoff over the canal.

10.name: Universal Express Pass 4 or 7 (SNW timed entry)
10.desc: USJ's Express Pass lets you skip the standby line on a set number of headline rides, and in August those standby lines run 90 to 180 minutes. Super Nintendo World needs its own timed-entry ticket on top, so pairing an Express Pass with the earliest SNW slot is how you actually get on Mario Kart, Yoshi, and the big coasters instead of baking in queues.
facts: SNW timed entry books via app, free with pass
Buy weeks ahead, peak dates sell out

10.name: Donkey Kong Country expansion
10.desc: This zone opened December 2024 and roughly doubled the size of Super Nintendo World with jungle theming, Kong statues, and hidden interactive gimmicks. The star is Mine-Cart Madness, a coaster that fakes a gap in the track so your cart looks like it jumps a broken rail. Early in the day the DK side runs shorter waits than the Mario area, so hit it first then loop back.
facts: Mine-Cart Madness is the signature coaster
Do DK side first, shorter morning lines
imgterms: Donkey Kong Country Universal Studios Japan

10.name: Power-Up Band + Bowser's Challenge AR run
10.desc: The Power-Up Band is a wristband that syncs to your USJ app and tracks coins and stamps as you punch question blocks and beat mini-games scattered around the land. Collect three keys from the mini-boss challenges and you unlock the final Bowser's Challenge, a competitive score battle. It turns the whole area into a dad-versus-son leaderboard, and Mario Kart: Bowser's Challenge itself uses AR headsets to steer and throw shells.
facts: Band needed to save mini-game scores
Three keys unlock the boss battle
Comes in Mario, Luigi, Peach and more

10.name: Night lap: Hollywood Dream / Flying Dinosaur + Dotonbori
10.desc: After sunset Super Nintendo World lights up neon and the whole Mario area glows, which is the best time for photos and short waits. Hollywood Dream is a smooth backwards-and-forwards launch coaster and Flying Dinosaur is a flying coaster that dangles you face-down over the park. Close the night with a train to Dotonbori for late takoyaki under the Glico running-man sign.
facts: Flying Dinosaur is a face-down flying coaster
USJ to Dotonbori approx 20 minutes by train
Late rides often have shortest waits

11.name: Super Potato Osaka + Retro TV Game Shop crawl
11.desc: Super Potato is the legendary retro-game chain, stacked floor to ceiling with boxed Famicom carts, loose Game Boy games, old controllers, and a retro arcade corner. The surrounding Nipponbashi strip, aka Den Den Town, is Osaka's answer to Akihabara but cheaper and less picked-over. It is a genuine hunting ground for CRT-era gaming loot rather than a tourist trap.
facts: Nearest stop: Nipponbashi or Ebisucho Station
Bring cash, small shops prefer it

11.name: Round1 Stadium 8 floors + VS Park
11.desc: Round1 Stadium in Namba is a multi-floor entertainment tower with UFO catcher claw machines, rhythm and fighting arcades, bowling, karaoke, and darts. The VS Park floor is Bandai Namco's active-play zone with full-body sports mini-games like sprinting against a virtual cheetah. It is built for a head-to-head dad-versus-son night that runs from claw machines to a rhythm-game showdown.
facts: VS Park is a timed-entry ticket
Bowling, arcade, karaoke under one roof

11.name: Pokemon Center Osaka + Jump Store + Mandarake Grand Chaos
11.desc: Pokemon Center Osaka sits inside Daimaru Umeda and carries region-exclusive plush and merch you cannot get abroad. Nearby the Shonen Jump flagship stocks One Piece, Dragon Ball, and Jujutsu Kaisen goods, while Mandarake Grand Chaos runs multiple floors of vintage figures, doujin, and out-of-print collectibles. Together they are the anime-merch trifecta of the Umeda district.
facts: Pokemon Center inside Daimaru Umeda, 13th floor
All three near Umeda / Osaka Station
Mandarake is best for vintage figures

11.name: Kuromon Market lunch (kept, but fast) → Gachapon Kaikan run
11.desc: Kuromon Ichiba is a covered market where stalls grill scallops, tuna, and wagyu skewers you eat on the spot. Do it fast, then hit a gachapon hall in Den Den Town where a wall of capsule-toy machines runs a few hundred yen a pull. It is the perfect low-stakes souvenir sweep: cheap, random, and endlessly re-playable.

12.name: Keep Hiroshima, lead with Miyajima
12.desc: Spend the morning at the Peace Memorial Park and Museum, then ferry out to Miyajima to see the great torii gate that appears to float at high tide. The island has free-roaming bowing deer and the Mount Misen ropeway climbs to viewpoints over the Seto Inland Sea. Leading with the heavy history and finishing on the island keeps the day from ending on a somber note.
facts: Osaka to Hiroshima approx 1.5 hrs shinkansen
Miyajima ferry approx 10 minutes each way
Misen ropeway approx 2,000 yen round trip
Check tide times for the floating torii

12.name: Swap for Himeji Castle (samurai fan's dream)
12.desc: Himeji sits directly on the Osaka-Hiroshima shinkansen line and is Japan's largest surviving original castle, never destroyed and never rebuilt in concrete. Its white keep and maze of defensive gates are the real-deal feudal fortress that a hundred anime and samurai films have copied. You can climb the original wooden interior all the way to the top floor.
facts: Osaka to Himeji approx 30-45 min shinkansen
UNESCO site, Japan's biggest original keep

12.name: Full Osaka stay-day: Shinsekai + Spa World + Tsutenkaku
12.desc: Skip the trains and stay local in Shinsekai, a retro district of neon backstreets built around the Tsutenkaku tower and packed with kushikatsu deep-fried skewer joints. Tsutenkaku has an observation deck and a spiral slide that drops you down the tower. Next door, Spa World is a giant bathhouse with European and Asian themed onsen floors for a father-son soak.
facts: Shinsekai is Osaka's retro neon district
Spa World day pass approx 1,500 yen

12.name: Second Universal half-day (Express evening ticket)
12.desc: If Super Nintendo World left you wanting more, USJ sells discounted twilight and after-4pm tickets that get you back in for the evening. Lines drop sharply late in the day, so you can mop up the coasters and rides you missed the first time with near-zero waits. It is the cheapest way to squeeze a second park visit into the trip.
facts: Twilight tickets cheaper than full day
SNW may still need timed entry

13.name: Osaka Castle exterior + Miraiza grounds (fast version)
13.desc: Osaka Castle's gold-trimmed keep rises over a wide moat and stone walls, and you can circle the grounds and get the money shot from the plaza in well under an hour. The Miraiza building at the base has restaurants, souvenirs, and free samurai-armor photo spots. Skip the museum line inside unless the flight is late and you want the elevator to the top deck.
facts: Grounds free, castle interior approx 600 yen
Miraiza has samurai armor photo ops

13.name: Kuromon or Dotonbori last market breakfast
13.desc: Make the last meal count with a final round of street food: takoyaki, fresh uni and scallops at Kuromon, or a farewell bowl of tonkotsu ramen in Dotonbori. Both spots let you graze stall to stall so you can hit the exact things you will crave on the flight home. Go early before the lunch crowds fill the arcades.
facts: Dotonbori is a 10-min walk from Namba

13.name: Don Quijote Dotonbori + Ebisu Tower ferris wheel
13.desc: Don Quijote in Dotonbori is a chaotic multi-floor discount store crammed with snacks, exclusive Kit-Kat flavors, cosmetics, gadgets, and last-minute souvenirs. Bolted to the building is the Ebisu Tower, an oval-shaped ferris wheel that gives one final ride over the neon canal. It is the ultimate one-stop souvenir dump before the airport.
facts: Open very late, good for last-minute runs

13.name: Final gachapon + Pokemon Center loot run
13.desc: Blow the last of your loose yen coins on a wall of gachapon capsule machines, then swing by Pokemon Center Osaka in Daimaru Umeda for the region-exclusive merch you can only get here. It is a low-stakes, high-fun closer that turns leftover change into souvenirs. Time it so you finish with enough buffer to reach the airport.
facts: Save yen coins for the machines

1.name: GiGO / Taito Station Kabukicho
1.desc: A multi-floor Sega-lineage arcade a minute from Omoide Yokocho, stacked with UFO catcher crane games, rhythm cabinets like maimai and Chunithm, and fighting-game setups. You feed 100-yen coins in, chase a prize plush, and get your first jetlagged taste of Japanese arcade culture without needing to think hard. Ground floors are all claw machines, upper floors get serious with music and versus games.

1.name: Godzilla Head, Hotel Gracery Kabukicho
1.desc: A life-size Godzilla head juts out over the Toho Cinemas building in the heart of Kabukicho and roars with lights and smoke on the hour after dark. It is free, gloriously stupid, and the definitive first-night Tokyo photo. Stand in the plaza below or grab a view from the 8th-floor cafe terrace.

1.name: Shibuya Sky at night
1.desc: An open-air rooftop observation deck 229 meters up over Shibuya Crossing, with a glass-edge sky corner and hammocks under the stars. The night slot delivers the full sea-of-neon skyline with Tokyo Tower and Skytree glowing in the distance. Book the roughly 9pm timed entry online in advance because sunset slots sell out fast.

1.name: Karaoke Kan, Shibuya
1.desc: The karaoke building made famous by Lost in Translation, where you get a private soundproof room instead of a public stage. The song books are loaded with anime openings, game themes, and Western hits, and no drinking is required to have fun. It is the perfect low-pressure ice-breaker for dad and son after a long flight.

2.name: Street Kart Asakusa (real-road go-karting)
2.desc: A guided go-kart tour on actual public streets, cruising past Senso-ji temple and along the Sumida River in a costume of your choice. Dad races son through real Tokyo traffic while a guide leads and GoPro footage gets captured for you. It is the single most unbelievable I-cannot-believe-this-is-legal thing on the trip.

2.name: Nakano Broadway
2.desc: A retro shopping complex four floors deep in Mandarake stores selling vintage figures, retro Famicom carts, anime cels, and rare trading cards. It is grittier, cheaper, and far less touristy than Akihabara, aimed at real collectors. A quick JR ride from Shinjuku and an easy swap for the pricey Skytree deck.

2.name: Pokemon Center + Nintendo goods, Skytree Town (Solamachi)
2.desc: If you keep Skytree on the plan, skip the expensive observation deck and hit the character shops at its base instead. The Tokyo Solamachi mall houses a Pokemon Center packed with plush and exclusives plus other Nintendo and anime goods stores. You get the Skytree photo from the ground and spend the money on merch.

2.name: Samurai Ninja Museum, Asakusa
2.desc: A hands-on museum where you wear real-weight armor, throw ninja stars at targets, and swing a katana in a short guided lesson. It leans cheesy but in the most fun way and sits a short walk from Senso-ji. The photo set of dad and son in full samurai gear basically sells itself.

3.name: Nintendo Tokyo + Pokemon Center Shibuya (Parco 6F)
3.desc: Japan's only official Nintendo store sits on the 6th floor of Shibuya Parco alongside a Pokemon Center and a Capcom Store. You get exclusive Mario, Zelda, and Splatoon merch, giant photo installations, and Street Fighter goods all on one floor. For a gamer this floor is basically a pilgrimage.

3.name: Jump Shop, Shibuya
3.desc: The official Shonen Jump store stocked with One Piece, Dragon Ball, Demon Slayer, and Jujutsu Kaisen merch you cannot buy back home. Expect exclusive figures, apparel, and manga-tie-in goods across every big franchise. A must-stop if he grew up on Jump anime.

3.name: Ura-Harajuku / Cat Street thrift + sneaker crawl
3.desc: The back-alleys behind Harajuku's main drag, where BAPE, atmos, WEGO, Kinji vintage, and Kailua cluster along Cat Street. This is where actual Tokyo teens shop for streetwear and sneakers, not the tourist candy of Takeshita Street. Budget a couple of hours to dig through racks and hunt limited kicks.

3.name: Shimokitazawa detour
3.desc: Two train stops from Shibuya, Shimokita is Tokyo's vintage-clothing capital, threaded with tiny record shops, retro game stores, and mellow cafes. The vibe is low-key and creative, all narrow lanes and secondhand racks. Add it if he is into thrifting streetwear or hunting cheap records.
facts: ~3 min from Shibuya by Keio line

4.name: Hey (Hirose Entertainment Yard)
4.desc: A legendary hardcore Akihabara arcade famous for its floor of vertical shoot-em-ups plus Street Fighter and Guilty Gear cabinets full of serious local players. This is where the real Akiba fighting-game and shmup scene lives, not the tourist claw floors. Watch a top player run a bullet-hell board and you will get why it is a landmark.

4.name: Mandarake Complex + Radio Kaikan
4.desc: Mandarake Complex packs eight floors of used figures, manga, cels, and retro game carts, while nearby Radio Kaikan is a tower of figure and model shops. Together they are the retro-hunting endgame, going far deeper than Super Potato alone. Give it a couple of hours and expect to find something you have never seen for sale.

4.name: Yodobashi Akiba + BEEP retro PC basement
4.desc: Yodobashi Akiba is a nine-floor electronics, gunpla, and game monster where you can lose hours in the model kit and gaming floors. BEEP nearby is a basement shrine to retro PCs, dojin games, and oddball vintage hardware. One is a mega-store, the other a deep-cut collector cave, and both are steps from the station.

4.name: Night add-on: gachapon hall + Akiba neon + arcade tournament
4.desc: After dark hit the GiGO gachapon floors, where hundreds of capsule machines line the walls for a few hundred yen a pull. Then wander the neon-lit main drag, which is the real Akihabara photo, and check whether a weekend fighting-game tournament is running at one of the arcades. It turns the district into an evening playground.

5.name: teamLab Planets, Toyosu
5.desc: A barefoot, walk-through digital art museum where you wade through knee-deep water and step into rooms of light and projection. The infinity mirror crystal room and the floating-flower dome are the standout shots that flood everyone's camera roll. You physically walk into the art rather than just look at it.

5.name: Unicorn Gundam Statue + Gundam Base, DiverCity Odaiba
5.desc: A 20-meter life-size Unicorn Gundam outside DiverCity Odaiba that transforms between modes with a light-and-sound show after dark. Inside, Gundam Base is the largest gunpla store on earth, wall to wall with model kits including shop exclusives. For an anime and mecha fan this is non-negotiable.

5.name: Tokyo Joypolis, Odaiba
5.desc: Sega's indoor theme park stacked with VR rides, arcade racers, and motion coasters all under one roof. It is fully rainy-day-proof and built for dad-versus-son bragging rights across dozens of attractions. Grab a passport ticket if you plan to ride everything.

5.name: Miraikan (National Museum of Emerging Science)
5.desc: A hands-on future-tech museum showcasing robotics, the legacy of ASIMO, and a giant suspended OLED globe called Geo-Cosmos. Exhibits let you poke at AI, space, and biotech in interactive stations. Swap it in if he leans more tech-nerd than crowds and shopping.
